subtopics.title: Fundamentals of Organic Chemistry
common_misconceptions: Organic chemistry only studies living things (studies carbon compounds)
All carbon compounds are organic (CO₂, carbonates, carbides are inorganic exceptions)
Isomers have different properties (physical may differ, chemical similar if same functional group)
subtopics.teaching_note: Organic chemistry: study of carbon compounds (except CO, CO₂, carbonates, carbides). Carbon's unique properties: catenation (forms chains/rings), tetravalency, forms covalent bonds with C, H, O, N, halogens. Homologous series: group of compounds with same functional group, each differing by CH₂. Members show gradual change in physical properties, similar chemical properties. Functional groups determine chemical behavior. Types of formulae: molecular (C₂H₆), structural (CH₃CH₃), displayed (showing all bonds).

subtopics.title: Alkanes
common_misconceptions: Alkanes are unreactive (they undergo combustion and substitution)
All alkanes are gases (C1-C4 gases, C5-C17 liquids, C18+ solids)
Cracking produces only smaller alkanes (produces alkenes too)
subtopics.teaching_note: Alkanes: saturated hydrocarbons (only single C-C bonds). General formula: CnH₂n₊₂. Sources: natural gas (methane), petroleum (crude oil). Reactions: Combustion (complete: CO₂ + H₂O; incomplete: CO + C). Substitution: with halogens in UV light. Cracking: thermal decomposition of long-chain alkanes to shorter alkanes + alkenes. Uses: fuels (petrol, diesel, kerosene, cooking gas). Nigerian context: crude oil from Niger Delta, refined at Port Harcourt, Warri, Kaduna refineries.

subtopics.title: Alkenes and Alkynes
common_misconceptions: Alkenes only undergo addition (also polymerization, oxidation)
Bromine water test only works for alkenes (works for any unsaturated compound)
Alkynes are more reactive than alkenes
subtopics.teaching_note: Alkenes: unsaturated, C=C double bond. General formula: CnH₂n. Test for unsaturation: decolorizes bromine water (red-brown → colorless), decolorizes acidified KMnO₄ (purple → colorless). Addition reactions: hydrogenation (H₂, Ni catalyst → alkane), halogenation (Br₂ → dibromoalkane), hydration (H₂O, H₂SO₄ → alkanol). Polymerization: ethene → polyethene (polythene). Alkynes: C≡C triple bond. General formula: CnH₂n₋₂. Ethyne (acetylene) used in oxy-acetylene welding.

subtopics.title: Alcohols
common_misconceptions: All alcohols are safe to drink (methanol is poisonous)
Primary, secondary, tertiary refer to number of OH groups (refer to carbon bearing OH)
Fermentation produces pure ethanol (produces ~15% max, needs distillation)
subtopics.teaching_note: Alcohols: contain -OH (hydroxyl) functional group. Classification: Primary (1°): -CH₂OH (oxidizes to aldehyde then carboxylic acid). Secondary (2°): >CHOH (oxidizes to ketone). Tertiary (3°): >COH (resistant to oxidation). Ethanol production: fermentation (enzymes break glucose to ethanol + CO₂, 35-55°C) and hydration of ethene (H₂SO₄ catalyst, 300°C, 60-70 atm). Dehydration: ethanol → ethene + H₂O (conc H₂SO₄, 170°C). Uses: solvents, fuels, beverages (palm wine in Nigeria), antiseptics, sterilization.

subtopics.title: Electrolysis
common_misconceptions: Electrolytes conduct electricity in solid state (need to be molten or aqueous)
Anode is always positive (positive in electrolysis, negative in galvanic cells)
The mass deposited is proportional to voltage (it's proportional to charge/current × time)
subtopics.teaching_note: Electrolysis: decomposition of compound by electric current. Electrolyte: substance that conducts electricity when molten/aqueous (ionic compound). At cathode (negative): cations gain electrons (reduction). At anode (positive): anions lose electrons (oxidation). Faraday's 1st law: mass deposited ∝ charge passed (m = ZIt where Z = electrochemical equivalent). Faraday's 2nd law: same charge deposits masses proportional to chemical equivalents. 1 Faraday = 96,500 C = 1 mole of electrons. Applications: extraction of aluminium, electroplating (chromium, silver, gold), purification of copper.
